questions.question: Which protein forms the thick filaments in skeletal muscle?
The human axial skeleton consists of:

options.B: Skull, vertebral column, sternum and ribs

questions.question: Amoeboid movement is facilitated by:

options.A: Actin and myosin in muscle fibres

questions.question: The sliding filament theory of muscle contraction was proposed by:
Osteoporosis is characterized by:

options.C: Reduced bone mineral density leading to brittle bones
options.D: Deposition of uric acid in joints

questions.question: Which type of joint allows maximum range of motion?
The diagram shows a sarcomere during contraction. What happens to the I-band and H-zone?
questions.diagram_description: Three diagrams of sarcomere: (A) relaxed state – wide I-band (actin only), wide H-zone (myosin only), full A-band; (B) partially contracted – I-band narrowed, H-zone narrowed, A-band unchanged; (C) fully contracted – I-band almost disappeared, H-zone disappeared, A-band unchanged, Z-lines very close together

options.B: Both I-band and H-zone decrease; A-band unchanged

questions.question: Troponin-C binds to:
The number of vertebrae in the human vertebral column is:
Rheumatoid arthritis is:

options.C: Gout due to uric acid deposition

questions.question: The diagram shows the neuromuscular junction. What is released from the motor nerve terminal to trigger muscle contraction?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of neuromuscular junction showing: motor neuron axon terminal (synaptic knob), synaptic vesicles containing acetylcholine, synaptic cleft, motor end plate (sarcolemma with acetylcholine receptors nicotinic type), muscle fibre below. Steps shown: action potential → Ca2+ entry → vesicle fusion → ACh release → ACh binds receptors → depolarization of sarcolemma → muscle action potential → contraction

questions.question: Gout is caused by deposition of:
The pectoral girdle consists of:
Motor units consisting of fast-twitch glycolytic fibres are used for:
Which structure prevents hyperextension of the knee?
The diagram shows the cross-bridge cycle in muscle contraction. At which step does ATP hydrolysis (ATPase) occur?
questions.diagram_description: Cross-bridge cycle diagram: (1) Myosin head cocked (high energy) – ADP+Pi attached; (2) Cross-bridge attachment – myosin head binds to actin; (3) Power stroke – myosin head pivots, ADP+Pi released, filaments slide; (4) Rigor state – myosin remains attached; (5) ATP binds myosin – cross-bridge detaches; (6) ATP hydrolysis re-cocks myosin head. Arrows connecting steps

options.C: When ATP binds and myosin detaches, re-cocking the head

questions.question: Cilia movement is produced by:
The human skull has how many bones in total?
Muscular dystrophy (Duchenne type) is caused by:

options.B: Mutation in dystrophin gene on X chromosome

questions.question: Which mineral is stored in bones and released when blood levels drop?
The diagram shows different types of joints. Identify the synovial joint type found at the shoulder.
questions.diagram_description: Diagrams of joint types: (A) Fibrous (suture) – no cavity, bones connected by fibrous tissue (skull); (B) Cartilaginous – slight movement, fibrocartilage disc between bones (vertebrae); (C) Hinge – uniaxial movement (elbow, knee); (D) Ball and socket – multiaxial, spherical head in cup-shaped socket (shoulder – humerus in glenoid cavity, hip – femur in acetabulum)

questions.question: Sarcoplasmic reticulum in muscle fibre stores:
The tibia and fibula form part of:
Tendinitis is inflammation of:
The power stroke in muscle contraction occurs when:

options.B: Myosin head pivots after releasing ADP and Pi

questions.question: The diagram shows a cross-section of skeletal muscle with different fibre types stained. Which fibre type (Type I) is fatigue-resistant?
questions.diagram_description: Cross-section of skeletal muscle histochemical stain showing: Type I fibres (slow oxidative – dark stain, small diameter, many mitochondria, high myoglobin – red, used for endurance); Type IIa fibres (fast oxidative glycolytic – intermediate stain, medium diameter); Type IIb/IIx fibres (fast glycolytic – light stain, large diameter, few mitochondria, fatigue quickly – used for power). Labelled with properties

questions.question: Oxygen debt in muscles after exercise is repaid by:

options.B: Oxidizing lactic acid back to pyruvate or glycogen in liver

questions.question: Pivot joint is found at:
The carpals are bones of:
Muscle cramps are caused by:

options.B: Sustained involuntary muscle contraction, often due to electrolyte imbalance

questions.question: The diagram shows the action of calcium in muscle contraction. Where does Ca2+ bind to initiate contraction?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing thin filament structure: actin monomers forming double helix, tropomyosin strands in grooves blocking myosin-binding sites, troponin complex (TnI, TnC, TnT) at regular intervals. In resting state: tropomyosin blocks sites. When Ca2+ released from SR, Ca2+ binds to TnC → conformational change → tropomyosin shifts → myosin binding sites exposed on actin

questions.question: The pelvic girdle consists of:

options.A: Two coxal bones (each formed by ilium, ischium, pubis)

questions.question: Calcitonin is secreted by the thyroid gland and it:

options.B: Decreases blood calcium by inhibiting osteoclasts

questions.question: Immovable joints (synarthrosis) are found in:
Which structure is damaged in a 'slipped disc'?
Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease in which:

options.B: Acetylcholine receptors at NMJ are destroyed by antibodies
options.D: Ca2+ channels in muscle are blocked

questions.question: The number of ribs in humans is:
The diagram shows bone remodeling. Which cells break down bone tissue (resorption)?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of bone remodeling cycle: Osteoclasts (large multinucleated cells, derived from monocytes) on bone surface creating resorption pit (Howship's lacunae), dissolving mineral by HCl and enzymes. Osteoblasts (single nucleus, derived from mesenchymal stem cells) depositing new osteoid (collagen + mineral). Osteocytes (mature osteoblasts trapped in lacunae) maintaining bone. RANKL/OPG signaling pathway shown

questions.question: Latent period in muscle contraction is:

options.A: Time from relaxation to next stimulus
options.B: Time between stimulus and start of contraction

questions.question: The total number of bones in the human body is:
Which type of movement do white blood cells use to move through tissues?
Actin filaments are anchored to which sarcomere structure?
Osteoarthritis is caused by:
The talus and calcaneus are bones of:
Rigor mortis occurs because:

options.B: ATP depletion after death prevents cross-bridge detachment
options.C: Ca2+ is rapidly pumped back into SR

questions.question: The diagram shows different types of fractures. Which fracture type is common in osteoporotic patients?
questions.diagram_description: Diagrams of fracture types: (A) Transverse – straight break across bone shaft; (B) Spiral – bone twisted, fracture spirals around shaft; (C) Comminuted – bone shattered into multiple fragments (common in elderly/osteoporotic bones due to brittle, low-density bone); (D) Greenstick – incomplete fracture, bone bends (children)

questions.question: Which ion is released from SR to initiate muscle contraction and pumped back to cause relaxation?
Cartilage differs from bone in that cartilage:
The knee joint is an example of:
The 'all or none' law of muscle contraction states:

options.B: A muscle fibre either contracts fully or not at all when adequately stimulated
options.D: Strength of contraction increases with stimulus strength
